coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of copper ions using coumarin-functionalized carbon dots based on FRET
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Bai, Zhangjun
Chen, Yuan
Zu, Fanlin
Li, Xing
Xu, Jinxia
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: A novel FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe (CMH-GA-CDs) was designed for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solution. The CMH-GA-CDs were prepared through grafting 7-diethylaminocoumarin-3-carbohydrazide (HCM) on the surface of glyoxylic acid-modified carbon dots (GA-CDs) by amidation reaction. In the absence of Cu2+, a FRET pair from the CDs to CMH units was established, in which the CMH-GA-CDs exhibited dual emission bands centered at 400 nm (CDs) and 458 nm(CMH), respectively, under optimal excitation wavelength of CDs (λex = 340 nm). Upon addition of 30 μM Cu2+ to CMH-GA-CDs solution, Cu2+ coordinated with the heteroatoms N and O, which caused the inhibition of FRET process and the change of fluorescence intensity ratio I458/I400 from 1.95 to 0.91. The ratio of I458/I400 exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–10 μM and the limit of detection was as low as 0.21 μM. Furthermore, The CMH-GA-CDs were used for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water and lake water with satisfactory recovery ratio between 91% and 104%, demonstrating the promising applicability for detecting Cu2+ in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Coumarin
Copper ions
Ratiometric
Fluorescence probe
FRET
Water
Samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ with the CMH-GA-CDs
Fluorescence detection of the CMH-GA-CDs to Cu2+
Possible mechanism of CMH-GA-CDs response to Cu2+
Determination of Cu2+ in practical water

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The design strategy of CMH-GA-CDs for detecting Cu2+.
The schematic illustration of the synthetic process of CMH-GA-CDs.
Schematic illustration for the CMH-GA-CDs detection of Cu2+.

(a) TEM images of CDs, inset: High-resolution TEM images of CDs; (b) Size distribution of CDs; (c) TEM images of CMH-GA-CDs, inset: High-resolution TEM images of CMH-GA-CDs; (d) Size distribution of CMH-GA-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of CDs, GA-CDs, CMH and CMH-GA-CDs; (b) XPS analysis of CDs, GA-CDs, and CMH-GA-CDs; (c) Zeta potential of CDs, GA-CDs, CMH and CMH-GA-CDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of the CDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs under excited at 340 nm (black line) and absorption spectra of the CMH (blue line); (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, GA-CDs, CMH and CMH-GA-CDs; (d) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, GA-CDs, CMH and CMH-GA-CDs under excited at 340 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Fluorescence response of the CMH-GA-CDs in the presence of different concentrations Cu2+; (b) Plot of fluorescence intensity ratio of I458 /I400 versus the concentration of Cu2+ (0–30 μM), inset: linear fitting curve of I458/I400 versus the concentration of Cu2+ (0–10 μM); (c) Fluorescence response of the CMH-GA-CDs in the presence of various metal ions; (d) Fluorescence response of the CMH-GA-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ with the addition of competitive metal ions.
Reaction time (a) and pH (b) influence on the fluorescence intensity ratio of I458 /I400 of the CMH-GA-CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+.
(a) Fluorescence reversibility of CMH-GA-CDs in the presence of 30 μM Cu2+ and 40 μM EDTA; (b) Absorbance spectra of the CMH-GA-CDs in the presence of various metal ions.
Detection of Cu2+ spiked in tap water and lake water samples.
Comparison of different CDs-based Cu2+ fluorescence probe.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of copper ions using coumarin-functionalized carbon dots based on FRET

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin, 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo, 0010024, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe (CMH-GA-CDs) was designed for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solution. The CMH-GA-CDs were prepared through grafting 7-diethylaminocoumarin-3-carbohydrazide (HCM) on the surface of glyoxylic acid-modified carbon dots (GA-CDs) by amidation reaction. In the absence of Cu2+, a FRET pair from the CDs to CMH units was established, in which the CMH-GA-CDs exhibited dual emission bands centered at 400 nm (CDs) and 458 nm(CMH), respectively, under optimal excitation wavelength of CDs (λex = 340 nm). Upon addition of 30 μM Cu2+ to CMH-GA-CDs solution, Cu2+ coordinated with the heteroatoms N and O, which caused the inhibition of FRET process and the change of fluorescence intensity ratio I458/I400 from 1.95 to 0.91. The ratio of I458/I400 exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–10 μM and the limit of detection was as low as 0.21 μM. Furthermore, The CMH-GA-CDs were used for the detection of Cu2+ in tap water and lake water with satisfactory recovery ratio between 91% and 104%, demonstrating the promising applicability for detecting Cu2+ in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ions (Cu2+), an essential element for living organisms, plays a critical role in physiological and pathological events, such as Alzheimer’s disease and Wilson’s disease [1]. Excess copper intake in living organisms can induce damage to the liver, kidney and central nervous system [2,3]. In addition, for the widespread use of copper in industry, the heavy metal contamination caused by Cu2+ has become an urgent environmental problem to be solved [4,5]. So, it is essential that the development of new methods for determining Cu2+ in biological samples and environmental water samples. The conventional methods for monitoring Cu2+ include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), capillary electrophoresis and voltammetry [6]. These techniques require complicated operation procedures, expensive instrumentations and large sample volumes [7]. In contrast, fluorescence detection methods for Cu2+ have been widely exploited and attracted increasing attention by researchers due to its high sensitivity, good selectivity and easy operation [8,9].

Currently, various fluorescent dyes and fluorescent nanoparticles were widely reported for the determination of Cu2+ such as coumarin, rhodamine, fluorescein, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), nanoclusters and carbon dots (CDs) [10–14]. The mechanisms of organic fluorescent probes for the detection of Cu2+ mainly contain two types [15,16]. One is the coordinated probe, its fluorescence is usually quenched by the paramagnetic of the Cu2+. The other is reactive probe, its fluorescence is quenched or enhanced by the catalytic hydrolysis of Cu2+. Especially, some specific organic fluorescent probes with similar bisamide structure were developed for sensitive and selective detecting Cu2+ [17] (Scheme 1 ). However, poor water solubility and biological toxicity limit their practical applications. Compared with organic fluorescent dyes and QDs, CDs possessed excellent water dispersibility, good cell permeability and biocompatibility [18,19]. By virtue of these superior properties, the CDs for the recognition of Cu2+ have been continuously explored [20,21]. Since most pristine CDs lack of excellent selectivity for specific identification of Cu2+, some functionalized-CDs had been reported for Cu2+ through the modification of organic molecules on the surface of CDs [22,23]. These organic molecules as recognition units can arise coordination reaction or chemical reaction with Cu2+, inducing or stimulating changes of fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Meanwhile, the current CDs-based probes for Cu2+ mainly are single-intensity-based probe based on fluorescence turn-on or fluorescence turn-off, which is usually affected by the interferences of the instrumental efficiencies and environmental conditions [24]. By contrast, ratiometric fluorescence probe can provide built-in correction for the environmental effects and reliable method for visualizing detection at the molecular level [25,26].

Inspired by the previous reported, a novel ratiometric fluorescent probe (CMH-GA-CDs) was designed, which endows with the capability of CMH-GA-CDs for the ratiometric detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solution (Scheme 1). Herein, CDs with hydroxyl groups were synthesized and covalently linked to glyoxylic acid molecules to form glyoxylic acid-modified carbon dots (GA-CDs). Then, 7-diethylaminocoumarin-3-carbohydrazide (CMH) were modified on the surface of GA-CDs to obtain fluorescence probe CMH-GA-CDs (Scheme 2 ). The mechanism of this probe for Cu2+ is that Cu2 + coordinated with the heteroatoms N and O [15–17], and then reducing the energy transfer efficiency from CDs to CMH units [27,28]. The experimental results displayed that CMH-GA-CDs possessed good sensitivity, excellent selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for the detection of Cu2+. The limit of detection was calculated can be reached to 0.21 μM, which was much lower than the maximum level of Cu2+ (20 μM) in drinking water by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Furthermore, the CMH-GA-CDs were successfully applied in tap water and lake water with satisfactory recovery results.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out by a JEM‒2011 microscope (JEOL, Japan) (at 200 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR37 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. Fuorescence spectra were recorded with an F-380 ﬂuorescence spectrophotometer from Tianjin Gangdong scientific and technological development Co., Ltd. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a TU‒1901 UV–vis spectrometer (Beijing Puxi Inc., China). Zeta potential were measured with Zetasizer nano ZS90 (Edinburgh, UK).

Anhydrous citric acid (CA, 99.5%), Tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane (Tris, 98%), 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS, 98%) andHexadecyl trimethyl ammonium Bromide (CTAB, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Inc. 4-(Diethylamino)salicylaldehyde (98%), glyoxylic acid (GA, 40%), diethylmalonate (99%), piperidine (99%), hydrazine hydrate (98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout all experiment.

CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method according to the previous report by our group [29]. Firstly, CA (5 mmol, 0.96 g) and Tris (5 mmol, 0.60 g) were dissolved in distilled water (30 mL). Next, the solution was transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (50 mL) preheated at 90 ℃ for 1 h and then heated at 200 °C for 6 h. When the reactors cooled to ambient temperature naturally, the deep-brown aqueous dispersion liquid formed was centrifuged at medium speed (4000 rpm/min) for 15 min in order to remove insoluble particulates. Then, the solution was subjected to dialysis (MW = 3500 Da) to remove unreacted reagent and small molecular species. Finally, CDs were dried in a freeze-drying oven (yield: 0.75 g, 48.1%).

GA-CDs were prepared according to a literature procedure with a slight modification [30]. A mixture of CDs (0.8 g), GA (1.46, 20 mmol) and CTAB (0.1 g) in 30 ml of CH3OH/cyclohexan (v/v = 1/2) solution was stirred at 60 °C for 6 h under N2 atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, the solid was obtained by rotary evaporation. Then, the crude product was purified by silica-column chromatography using methylene chloride and methanol as eluents. Finally, the GA-CDs were dried under vacuum at 50 °C overnight.

The 7-diethylaminocoumarin-3-carbohydrazide (CMH) was synthesized according to the reported method [31]. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO) δ 9.46 (s, 1 H), 8.64 (s, 1 H), 7.66 (t, J = 12.0 Hz, 1 H), 6.78 (dd, J = 9.0, 2.2 Hz, 1 H), 6.58 (d, J = 2.1 Hz, 1 H), 4.63 (s, 2 H), 3.47 (q, J = 6.9 Hz, 4 H), 1.28 – 1.07 (m, 6 H). Briefly, GA-CDs (0.5 g), CMH (0.135 g, 0.5 mmol) and EDC (0.96 g, 0.5 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL DMF. After stirring for 0.5 h, the NHS (0.58 g, 0.5 mmol) was added in above mixture. The mixture was stirred and refluxed for 8 h under N2 atmosphere. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation. The obtained solid was purified by silica-gel column chromatography with methylene chloride and methanol as eluents. Finally, CMH-GA-CDs were dried under vacuum at 50 °C overnight.

The fluorescence quantum yield (φx) of CDs was defined by slope method with the reference of quinine sulfate whose QY is about 54.2%: compared the integrated photoluminescence intensity (λex = 340 nm) and the absorbance value (several values gave the curve) of the CDs samples with quinine sulfate. The φx was obtained by equation [21] φx = φst (Kx/Kst) (ηx/ηst)2, where φ is the fluorescence quantum yield, K is the slope determined by the curves and η is the refractive index. The subscript “st” refers to quinine sulfate and “x” refers to CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cu2+ with the CMH-GA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solution of the CMH-GA-CDs with concentration of 2 mg mL−1 was prepared in DMSO. Various metal ions stock solutions (including: Cu2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Co2+, Ce2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, As3+) were prepared at the concentration (0.002 mol/L). The sensitivity experiments were carried out by adding different concentrations of Cu2+ (0–30 μM) with 100 μL CMH-GA-CDs and 10 mL of HEPES buffer solutions (pH 7.4). The selectivity experiments were conducted by adding 200 μL metal ions and 100 μL CMH-GA-CDs into 10 mL HEPES buffer solutions (pH 7.4). The competition experiments were investigated by mixing 100 μL CMH-GA-CDs solutions, 200 μL other metal ion and 200 μL Cu2+ into 10 mL HEPES buffer solutions (pH 7.4). The mixed solution was incubated for 10 min before spectral measurements. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 340 nm (using 5/10 nm slit width).

To construct the ratiometric probe for detecting Cu2+ in water samples, it was a prerequisite that CDs presented high fluorescence quantum yields and excellent dispersion in water. In this work, Tris was selected as precursor of hydroxyl-terminated CDs due to its hydroxyl-rich structure. The CDs were prepared with CA and Tris through the two-step heating hydrothermal treatment. Firstly, some oligomer had been formed when the intermolecular dehydration and hydrothermal polymerization happened. Then, with the increase of temperature, the carbon nuclei grew with the further carbonization and formed CDs nanocrystalline. The −OH, as self-passivation layer, was introduced to the surface of CDs, which endowed the CDs with good hydrophilic performance. Subsequently, the GA moiety as the linker was modified on the surface of CDs to form GA-CDs. Furthermore, the CMH was further modified on the surface of GA-CDs to give CMH-GA-CDs by a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction (Scheme 2).

To confirm successful synthesis of CMH-GA-CDs, the morphology and size of the CDs and CMH-GA-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, CDs exhibited a quasi-spherical structure. Its size distribution was around 2–5 nm and the average particle size was 3.37 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c showed the TEM image of synthetic CMH-GA-CD, it can clear observed that the average size of CMH-GA-CDs was larger than CDs. The average size of CMH-GA-CDs was 4.2 nm and the size distribution was around 2.5–6 nm (Fig. 1d). In addition, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset: Fig. 1a and c), displayed that the CDs and CMH -GA-CDs had certain crystallinity with the average lattices of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the typical interlayer distance of graphene (101, 0.21 nm) [32]. It indicated that the size of CDs was changed while the morphology of CDs was invariant after modified by GA and CMH.

To further ascertain the successful synthesis of the CMH-GA-CDs, 1H NMR spectra, FT-IR spectra, XPS analysis and Zeta-potential measurements were used to characterize CDs, GA-CDs and CMH-GA-CDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the 1H NMR of CDs showed the methylene (around 3.4 ppm) and the hydroxyl groups (around 5.2 ppm), implying the precursor of Tris remain on the surface of CDs. In addition, the 1H NMR of GA-CDs displayed that hydroxyl groups at 5.2 ppm disappeared and new peak appeared at 2.3 ppm (Fig. S2). Fig. S3 presented that compound CMH were synthesized. Compared with GA-CDs, the resonance peaks at 7.1 and 7.5 ppm derived from the benzene ring were displayed in the 1H NMR spectrum of CMH-GA-CDs, indicating the successful surface modification of GA-CDs with the CMH (Fig. S4). Subsequently, as shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs showed broad absorption band at 3440 cm−1, which demonstrated the formation OH functional groups. A sharp peak at 1643 cm−1 could be assigned to amide bonding (CONH), indicating that a carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction occurred during the formation of the CDs. Compared to CDs, GA-CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peak of the COOH at 1747 cm−1. It suggested that GA had been successfully modified to the surface of CDs. CMH-GA-CDs displayed typically absorption peaks of OH and NH, which was originated from GA-CDs and CMH, respectively. The results indicate the successful synthesis of CMH-GA-CDs. The survey full spectrum of XPS analysis was displayed in Fig. 2b. The CDs were composed of 63.5% C, 5.4% N and 31.1% O. The chemical composition of the GA-CDs were 57.5% C, 4.3% N and 38.2% O. For CMH-GA-CDs, corresponding content of each element of C, N, and O were 59.3%, 6.2% and 34.5%. The change of elemental content can be attributed to the successful introduction of GA and CMH to the surface of CDs. In addition, the ζ-potential was used to further verify the generation of CMH-GA-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, due to the presence of −COOH on the surface of GA-CDs, the zeta potential of CDs increased from −8.09 mV to −15.6 mV. Meanwhile, the zeta potential of CMH-GA-CDs was measured as −11.2 mV due to the modifying CMH to the surface of GA-CDs.

The fluorescence emission behavior of the CD as an energy donor was first investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, like most other reported articles, a red-shift in the emission of CDs was observed with the excitation wavelength range from 280 to 380 nm, which can be associated to different sizes and emissive sites of the CDs [33–35]. The CDs displayed the highest fluorescent emission at 400 nm under a single excitation wavelength of 340 nm. In this case, the φx was calculated to be 62% by using quinine sulfate as the reference sample. Based on these behavior, the CDs can be chosen as an excellent energy donor in CDs based FRET probes. For CMH, it had maximum absorption at 415 nm. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the emission spectrum of CDs showed a large overlap with the absorbance of the CMH. Thus, a feasible FRET pair from CDs to CMH was theoretically constructed.

To further prove the formation of the CMH-GA-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence emission spectra were investigated in HEPES buffer solution (pH = 7.4). As shown in Fig. 3c, the CDs displayed a strong absorption peak at 328 nm, which was ascribed to the n-π* transitions of surface states [36]. For GA-CDs, a new absorption peak appeared at 357 nm. It can be explained that carboxyl -terminated GA-CDs has a higher surface oxidation degree than the hydroxyl-terminated CDs, resulting in the red shift of absorption position [37]. The CMH exhibited two absorption peaks at 258 and 419 nm. For the CMH-GA-CDs, absorption peak of GA-CDs at 357 nm disappeared and a new absorption peak was observed at 436 nm. It indicated that the CMH was successfully incorporated on the surface of GA-CDs. The fluorescence spectra were shown in Fig. 3d. The emission wavelengths of the CDs (λem = 398 nm), GA-CDs (λem = 416 nm), CMH (λem = 475 nm) were obtained under single excitation wavelength at 340 nm, respectively. Notably, CMH-GA-CDs displayed unique dual emission peaks at 400 nm (CDs) and 458 nm (CMH) when excited at 340 nm. It further indicated that CMH-GA-CDs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of the CMH-GA-CDs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence detection of Cu2+ was carried out upon addition of various common metal ions in HEPES buffer (pH = 7.4) under single excitation wavelength at 340 nm. To demonstrate the usability of CMH-GA-CDs for sensitive detection of Cu2+, the fluorescence response of the CMH-GA-CDs to different concentrations of Cu2+ was performed in Fig. 4 a. It was observed that fluorescence intensity gradually increased at 400 nm and rapidly decreased at 458 nm with the increasing of Cu2+. Correspondingly, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I458/I400 was continuously decreased with the concentration of Cu2+ from 0 to 30 μM (Fig. 4b). A good linear relationship was obtained in the range of 0–10 μM with correlation coefficient of 0.994 (inset: Fig. 4b). The limit of detection was calculated to be 0.21 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3, which was much lower than the maximum concentration (20 μM) of Cu2+ for drinking water permitted by the EPA. Subsequently, the selectivity was evaluated by parallel measuring the fluorescence response of the CMH-GA-CDs to other metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4c, the ratio I458/I400 was almost unchanged with the additions of 30 μM Na+, K+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+, As3+, and obviously decreased by 30 μM Cu2+. Furthermore, to assess the anti-interference ability of CMH-GA-CDs towards Cu2+, the competition experiments were further implemented. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the ratio of I458/I400 was almost unchanged in the presence of 30 μM other metal ions, and remarkable decrease was observed when 30 μM Cu2+ was added to the CMH-GA-CDs solution containing other metal ions. Thus, the above results suggested that CMH-GA-CDs possessed good sensitivity, excellent selectivity and strong anti-interference ability for the detection of Cu2+.

To study the optimal conditions of CMH-GA-CDs for Cu2+, the suitable reaction time and pH range for Cu2+ detection were studied. The ratio of I458/I400 remained almost unchanged within 30 min. Meanwhile, after adding 30 μM Cu2+ within 10 min, the ratio of I458/I400 rapidly decreased and then kept stable (Fig. 5 a). In addition, no remarkable fluctuation was observed between pH 3.0 and 9.0 in the absence of Cu2+. Upon addition of Cu2+, the ratio of I458/I400 displayed obvious decrease, which remained fairly stable from pH 5.0 to 9.0 (Fig. 5b). Therefore, the optimal reaction time and pH span were determined as 10 min and 5.0–9.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CMH-GA-CDs response to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the detection process, the possible detection mechanism was discussed. As shown in Fig. 6 a, after 30 μM Cu2+ was added to the CMH-GA-CDs solution and incubated for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity ratio I458/I400 varied from 1.95 to 0.91. Subsequently, 40 μM EDTA was added in above solution for 30 min, the ratio I458/I400 was restored from 0.91 to 1.84 and the recovery rate was as high as 94%. It demonstrated that the detection process of the CMH-GA-CDs for Cu2+ was reversible, and the CMH-GA-CDs belonged to coordinated probe. In addition, the fluorescence emissions of CDs, GA-CDs and CMH remained almost unchanged with the increasing of Cu2+ from 0 to 30 μM (Figs. S5, S6 and S7). Subsequently, when 100 μM various metal ions were added into the CMH-GA-CDs solution, only Cu2+ caused decrease of the absorption peak of CMH units at 435 nm (Fig. 6b). It reduced the overlap between the emission band of the CDs and the absorption of the CMH units, thereby the energy transfer efficiency declined. These phenomenon can be explained: after grafting CMH on the surface of CDs, FRET process from CDs to CMH was established; Then, the FRET process was inhibited upon the addition of Cu2+; As a result, the fluorescence intensity at 400 nm increased and at 458 nm decreased. The related detection mechanism was depicted in Scheme 3 . It was observed that the solution changed from pale-yellow to yellow under visible light and from blue-green to blue under UV light in the absence and presence of Cu2+.

To further confirm the occurrence of the FRET mechanism in the CMH-GA-CDs system, the Förster distance (R0 ), energy transfer efficiency (E), the distance between the acceptor and the donor (r) were calculated by equation as follows [27]:
(1)
R 0
= 0.211
(
k 2
n
- 4
φ X
J ( λ ) )
1 6
(2)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
=
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
r 6
(3)
J ( λ ) =
∫ n ∞
F D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ 4
d λ
Where k2 denotes the relative orientation in space of the transition dipoles of the donor and acceptor (=2/3), n is the refractive index of the medium (=1.33), φx is the fluorescence quantum yield of the donor (i.e., CDs) in the absence of acceptor (=0.62). F and F0 express the intensity of the nanoprobe before and after addition of 30 μM Cu2+; R0 represents the Forster distance at which the transfer efficiency E = 50%; r denotes the distance between energy donor and acceptor; J(λ) describes the spectral overlapping area of the emission of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor (i.e. CMH); FD(λ) is the donor normalized fluorescence emission spectrum, εA(λ) is the acceptor molar absorption coefficient, and λ is the wavelength.
According to Eqs. (1)–(3), we obtained that R0 = 1.45 nm, E = 22% and r = 1.79 nm. R0 and r were in the range of 1–10 nm, which was consistent with requirements for FRET mechanism [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ in practical water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method, two water samples from tap water (Tianjin, China) and lake water (Tianjin, China) were collected for the determination of Cu2+. Two water samples were filtered through 0.5 μM filter membrane to remove large suspended particulates, and then added Cu2+ with concentration of 2, 5 and 8 μM. After incubating with 10 min, the fluorescence test was conducted under the same conditions. As illustrated in Table 1 , the recoveries of Cu2+ varied in the range of 91%–104%. It suggested that CMH-GA-CDs have good effect on the detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

Furthermore, the performance of CMH-GA-CDs for Cu2+ detection was compared with those recently reported method (Table 2 ). Compared to other reports, CMH-GA-CDs had larger linear ranges. In addition, the detection limit of the 7 methods in Table 2 were below than the U. S. EPA regulations, but the most other methods existed some drawbacks in practical applications due to low fluorescent quantum yield, narrow pH detection span and poor selectivity. Thus, the methods exhibited potential advantage for the determination of Cu2+ in real samples.

A novel FRET probe CMH-GA-CDs was established by covalently linking CMH-functionalized CDs for the detection of Cu2+. In the absence of Cu2+, energy transfer occurred from CDs to CMH units. The CMH-GA-CDs displayed dual emission bands centered at 400 nm (CDs) and 458 nm (CMH), respectively, under optimal excitation wavelength of CDs at λex = 340 nm. After the addition of Cu2+ within 10 min, the process of energy transfer was inhibited, which resulted in enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of CDs and decreased of the fluorescence intensity of CMH units. The CMH-GA-CDs exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and strong anti-interference toward Cu2+ over other metal ions. A linear relationship can be obtained in the concentration range of 0–10 μM, and detection limit can be reached to 0.21 μM. This methods were further applied for determination of Cu2+ in tap water and lake water with satisfactory recovery ratio between 91% and 104%.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51678409, 51638011 and 51578375), Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507), and the Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (TD13-5042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.08.034.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrophobic-carbon-dot-based dual-emission micelle for ratiometric fluorescence biosensing and imaging of Cu2+ in liver cells
Graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for highly efficient detection of copper(II) ions
Ratiometric fluorescent paper sensor utilizing hybrid carbon dots-quantum dots for the visual determination of copper ions
A simple Schiff base fluorescence probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+
Quinoline based sensors for bivalent copper ions in living cells
Highly selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ with lysine enhancing bovine serum albumin modified-carbon dots fluorescent probe
Spirohydrazine rhodamine as a fluorescent chemodosimeter for the selective detection of Cu(II) ions and its application in live cell imaging
A facile fluorescent probe with a large Stokes shift for sequentially detecting copper and sulfide in 100% aqueous solution and imaging them in living cells
Fluorescent ion-imprinted sensor for selective and sensitive detection of copper (II) ions
Intrinsically fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for sensing Cu2+ in aqueous media and constructing an IMPLICATION logic gate
A fluorescence turn on assay for alkaline phosphatase based on the Cu2+ catalyzed Fenton-like reaction
Preparation of europium complex-conjugated carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of copper(ii) ions
Ratiometric, visual, dual-signal fluorescent sensing and imaging of pH/copper ions in real samples based on carbon dots-fluorescein isothiocyanate composites
Carbon-dot and quantum-dot-coated dual-emission core-satellite silica nanoparticles for ratiometric intracellular Cu2+ imaging
Highly sensitive and selective turn-on fluorescent and chromogenic probe for Cu2+ and ClO− based on a N-picolinyl rhodamine B-hydrazide derivative
A turn-on fluorescent rhodamine-acyl hydrazide for selective detection of Cu2+ ions
Synthesis and recognition properties for copper ions and cyanide anions of two coumarin hydrazide compounds
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
Carbon dots-bromoacetyl bromide conjugates as fluorescence probe for the detection of glutathione over cysteine and homocysteine
Constructing a fluorescent probe for specific detection of catechol based on 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid-functionalized carbon dots
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and visual probes for sensing copper ions in living cells via an electron transfer process
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells
Ratiometric fluorescence probes based on carbon dots
New fluorescent metal-ion detection using a paper-based sensor strip containing tethered rhodamine carbon nanodots
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Selective FRET-based sensing of 4-nitrophenol and cell imaging capitalizing on the fluorescent properties of carbon nanodots from apple seeds
Probing energy and Electron transfer mechanisms in fluorescence quenching of biomass carbon quantum dots
A novel multi-stimuli responsive gelator based on D-gluconic acetal and its potential applications
FRET-based rhodamine–coumarin conjugate as a Fe3+, selective ratiometric fluorescent sensor in aqueous media
Red-emissive carbon dots for fingerprints detection by spray method: coffee ring effect and unquenched fluorescence in drying process
Highly luminescent organosilane-functionalized carbon dots as a nanosensor for sensitive and selective detection of quercetin in aqueous solution
N-doped multi-fluorescent carbon dots for ‘turn off-on’ silver-biothiol dual sensing and mammalian cell imaging application
Carbon dots with red emission for sensing of Pt2+, Au3+, and Pd2+ and their bioapplications in vitro and in vivo
One-step microwave synthesis of carbon dots for highly sensitive and selective detection of copper ions in aqueous solution
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism
Silica-coated carbon dots conjugated to CdTe quantum dots: a ratiometric fluorescent probe for copper(II)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is an associate professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2007. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.
